answer:
In "Huzoor Risalat Ma'ab Mein" (In the Exalted Prophet's Presence), Iqbal depicts himself in a visionary audience with the Prophet Muhammad, who initiates the exchange with gentle, exalted praise, addressing the poet as the "Nightingale of Hijaz's garden-bower" whose "passionate song melts the heart of every flower" and whose "humble grace is the pride of a prostrated heart."[^1] Iqbal responds with profound deference, invoking "O Presence!" and humbly lamenting the spiritual failings of the modern world while offering a "phial of crystal bright" containing "the blood of Tripoli's martyrs" as a reverent gift reflective of the ummah's honor.[^2] The etiquette is one of utter veneration: courtly, poetic, and submissive, with no trace of criticism or equality—Iqbal positions himself as a supplicant before divine mercy.

By contrast, in "Sach keh doon ae Brahman! gar tu bura na maane" (O Brahmin, if you will not take offense), Iqbal opens with a courteous but conditional plea for tolerance—"O Brahmin, if you will not take offense"—before launching into pointed critique: the addressee's "ancient idols have lost reverence," they "hold your kin in scorn," and they mistakenly believe "your God is in a form of stone."[^3] The tone shifts toward fraternal invitation in the latter half, urging shared renewal ("Come, let us lift the veils of otherness anew") and a new "shrine" of universal love.[^4] The etiquette here is candid and advisory: polite at the outset to soften rebuke, boldly challenging orthodox beliefs, and ultimately reconciliatory, treating the Brahmin as an equal in need of reform rather than a figure of worship.

The comparison reveals a stark hierarchy in Iqbal's rhetorical posture. Toward the Prophet, etiquette embodies Islamic devotional protocol—elevated praise, self-effacement, and tribute—befitting a spiritual sovereign. Toward the Brahmin, it adopts a bolder, interfaith polemics: prefatory civility tempers direct admonition, reflecting Iqbal's reformist zeal to bridge Hindu-Muslim divides without subservience.[^5]

[^1]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 3*, ch. 11 "Huzoor Risalat Ma'ab Mein" ([translation/](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/11-huzoor-risalat-maab-mein/translation/#p-ae07ba)).

[^2]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 3*, ch. 11 "Huzoor Risalat Ma'ab Mein" ([translation/](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/11-huzoor-risalat-maab-mein/translation/#p-14a317)).

[^3]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 1*, ch. 36 "Sach keh doon ae Brahman! gar tu bura na maane" ([translation/](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/36-sach-keh-doon-ae-brahman-gar-tu-bura-na-maane/translation/#p-752d60)).

[^4]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 1*, ch. 36 "Sach keh doon ae Brahman! gar tu bura na maane" ([translation/](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/36-sach-keh-doon-ae-brahman-gar-tu-bura-na-maane/translation/#p-3a582d)).

[^5]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 1*, ch. 36 "Sach keh doon ae Brahman! gar tu bura na maane" ([translation/](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/36-sach-keh-doon-ae-brahman-gar-tu-bura-na-maane/translation/)).
